policy_statement: SFO will encourage and incentivize switching aviation sector fuel to low-carbon sources for air and ground fleets where viable (RPC.4-1).
mechanism_description: SFO will encourage and incentivize switching aviation sector fuel to low-carbon sources for air and ground fleets, reducing greenhouse gas emissions from aviation operations.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel/energy switching in transport → ↓ lifecycle fuel carbon intensity + ↓ combustion emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Aviation and airport ground operations shift energy inputs away from conventional fossil jet fuel/diesel/gasoline toward low-carbon aviation fuels (e.g., sustainable aviation fuel blends) and/or low-carbon ground fleet energy (renewable diesel, electricity, hydrogen where applicable).  
2) Mediating physical process: Lower carbon content and/or biogenic/renewable feedstock pathways (and potentially cleaner electricity) reduce net CO₂ released per unit of energy and can reduce upstream emissions in fuel production and distribution.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower lifecycle CO₂e emissions per flight/ground-vehicle mile (and potentially co-pollutants), yielding net GHG reductions from aviation-related fuel use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from aviation operations due to switching from fossil fuels to lower-carbon energy carriers.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Substituting aviation/ground fuels with lower-carbon alternatives reduces lifecycle GHG emissions from fuel combustion and supply chains.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The mechanism’s core outcome is direct emissions reduction (mitigation), not primarily reducing energy demand (resource efficiency), managing climate hazards (adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If “low-carbon” fuel were defined using non-climate criteria or had uncertain/negative lifecycle impacts (e.g., high ILUC biofuels), mitigation benefits could be reduced; however the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant effect remains GHG reduction via lower lifecycle carbon intensity.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that implements “low-carbon aviation fuel switching” is fundamentally an emissions-intensity reduction strategy for aviation energy use, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s best secondary characterization is “alternative fuels,” “electrification of ground support equipment,” “procurement,” or “pricing incentives” depends on policy-specific details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle emissions can vary widely by SAF pathway and electricity mix; some fuel pathways may have limited or contested net GHG benefits (e.g., indirect land-use change, upstream methane), but the mechanism’s dominant intent/effect is mitigation via lower lifecycle carbon intensity.
key_indicators: "encourage and incentivize"; "switching aviation sector fuel"; "low carbon sources"; "both air and ground fleets"; "where viable"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants (e.g., PM/NOx) and potential public health benefits from cleaner ground operations.
instance_edge_case_notes: Does not specify the incentive mechanism (e.g., rebates, fee differentials, grants, procurement incentives), so classification stays at a general incentive-program level rather than a pricing-mechanism or procurement-requirement.
